name: F2T2EA Kill Chain (Dynamic Targeting)
year_developed: 1990s (codified in Joint Publication 3-60 'Joint Targeting'; continuously updated; current edition reflects post-2001 operational experience)
progenitor: U.S. Joint Staff / Joint Forces Command; codified in Joint Publication (JP) 3-60; operationalized through the Joint Targeting School and Theater Air Ground System (TAGS)
driving_insight: The F2T2EA kill chain operationalizes time-sensitive targeting by recognizing that tactical speed is a function of structural coupling between intelligence, decision, and action nodes. Its central insight is that each link in the chain (Find, Fix, Track, Target, Engage, Assess) is a necessary condition for a successful engagement — if any single link is disrupted by an adversary or breaks due to friction, the entire engagement fails. The framework thus makes the chain's dependencies explicit, forcing planners to harden each link and compress the latency between links. Unlike OODA (which describes a cognitive cycle inside a commander's head), F2T2EA describes an organizational pipeline where different nodes (sensors, intelligence analysts, targeteers, commanders, weapon systems, BDA assets) execute different links, and handoffs between links are the primary points of failure. The framework's second insight is that the 'Kill Chain' is inherently fragile and adversary-aware: a competent enemy will deliberately attack the chain's weakest links (e.g., blinding sensors, jamming communications, masking signatures), making Kill Chain resilience as important as Kill Chain speed.
decision_style: hierarchical with delegated execution. The overall kill chain involves multiple decision points distributed across the chain. Find and Fix involve intelligence analyst decisions (is this a valid target?). Target involves the Joint Force Commander's delegated targeting authority deciding on engagement options. Engage involves the weapon system operator's tactical decision to execute the fire command. Assess involves a decision to re-attack or stand down. This distributed decision architecture reflects the military principle of centralized command with decentralized execution: the commander sets targeting priorities and rules of engagement (centralized), but individual engagement decisions are delegated to the lowest competent echelon (decentralized). The F2T2EA kills the OODA loop's single-pilot illusion — nobody 'owns' the whole chain; the chain succeeds or fails based on the quality of handoffs between decisions made at different echelons.
problem_class_targeted: F2T2EA targets dynamic (time-sensitive) targeting problems characterized by: (1) adversary-determined time windows — the target will not remain engageable indefinitely, so the chain must execute within a window dictated by enemy behavior; (2) incomplete initial information — the target is detected but not fully characterized, requiring progressive refinement through Fix and Track; (3) adversarial countermeasures — the enemy is actively trying to evade detection and engagement; (4) high-consequence decisions — a wrong engagement decision can result in civilian casualties, fratricide, or diplomatic incidents; (5) distributed execution — no single person sees the whole picture, requiring coordination across intelligence, operations, and fires functions. The problem class is well-defined (there is a specific target to engage), dynamic (target state and location change), and adversarial (enemy is actively resisting). F2T2EA is not designed for non-targeting problems and does not transfer cleanly to domains like software development or strategic planning where there is no 'target' to engage.
ordered_phases: Six sequential phases with iterative feedback. Phase 1 — FIND: sensors detect a potential threat; raw data is collected and an initial detection is validated to identify an emerging target of interest. Phase 2 — FIX: the target's identity, classification, and precise location are determined through sensor fusion and intelligence analysis; continuous sensor updates refine the picture until a valid detection is confirmed. Phase 3 — TRACK: the target is continuously tracked on sensor feeds while simultaneously assessing friendly force proximity and engagement feasibility to determine the 'target window' — the time and location envelope for a successful engagement. Phase 4 — TARGET: available engagement options (weapons, platforms, assets) are evaluated against the target window; targets are prioritized; the optimal attack option is selected, producing a final targeting solution. Phase 5 — ENGAGE: orders are transmitted through the command and control network; the fire command is executed; the selected weapon system attacks the target. Phase 6 — ASSESS: post-engagement battle damage assessment (BDA) is conducted through sensors and intelligence analysis; if the target is not defeated, a re-attack is authorized, looping back through the kill chain.
feedback_loops: Multiple explicit feedback loops. (1) ASSESS → FIND: if BDA shows the target was not defeated, re-attack is authorized, re-entering the chain at FIND (re-acquire the target in its new state). (2) TRACK → FIX: if tracking reveals that the initial fix was inaccurate or the target is maneuvering, tracking data feeds back to refine the fix. (3) TARGET → TRACK: if no viable engagement option exists within the current target window, the engagement is deferred and tracking continues until a new window opens. (4) ENGAGE → TARGET: if the engagement fails to achieve desired effects (weapon malfunction, target maneuvers, defenses defeat the attack), the targeting solution is revised. (5) Cross-chain intelligence: all phases feed into the common operational picture, enabling downstream phases to benefit from upstream refinements. These loops are explicitly designed into the doctrine, not emergent — JP 3-60 specifies re-attack procedures and target window management as doctrinal requirements.
gates: Multiple formal and informal gates exist between phases, each with specific pass criteria. (1) FIND → FIX gate: the initial detection must be validated — raw sensor contact must be confirmed as a valid target of interest before intelligence resources are committed to characterization. Validation criteria include: signal consistency, correlation with other sensors, and absence of known decoy signatures. (2) FIX → TRACK gate: the target must be positively identified and classified with sufficient confidence for engagement consideration; location accuracy must be within weapons delivery precision requirements. (3) TRACK → TARGET gate: a viable target window must exist — the target is within engagement range of available assets, friendly forces are not in the blast radius, and the engagement timeline is shorter than the window duration. This is the most complex gate, involving weaponeering, collateral damage estimation, and rules of engagement compliance. (4) TARGET → ENGAGE gate: the Commander's Decision — the Joint Force Commander (or delegated targeting authority) must approve the specific engagement. This is the primary Human-in-the-Loop gate. If the commander blocks, the target may be deferred (continue tracking), reassigned to a different asset, or placed on a Restricted Target List. (5) ENGAGE → ASSESS gate: the engagement must be completed and enough time must have elapsed for BDA sensors to collect post-strike data. (6) ASSESS → DONE gate: BDA confirms target defeat meeting the commander's desired effects threshold, OR re-attack is authorized. If BDA is inconclusive and no re-attack assets are available, the target is flagged for deliberate targeting in the next Joint Targeting Cycle iteration.
exit_criteria: The kill chain is 'done' when: (1) combat assessment confirms the target has been defeated to the commander's desired effects level (destruction, neutralization, suppression, or functional degradation); OR (2) the target window closes and cannot be reopened (target escapes, enters a sanctuary, is no longer operationally relevant); OR (3) commander rescinds targeting authority. Partial completion: a target may be functionally degraded but not destroyed, which may be 'done enough' depending on the desired effect. 'Done' is always defined by the commander's intent for that specific target — there is no universal done state. The Assess phase is the formal exit checkpoint: BDA + MEA + Reattack Recommendation either closes the loop or restarts it.
decomposition_method: The F2T2EA chain decomposes the targeting problem functionally by capability node rather than hierarchically or recursively. Each of the six phases represents a distinct functional capability that must exist in the force: sensors (Find), intelligence analysis (Fix), persistent surveillance (Track), targeteering and command decision (Target), weapons delivery (Engage), and battle damage assessment (Assess). This is a dependency-driven decomposition: each phase depends on the output of the previous phase, and the chain is only as strong as its weakest link. Within each phase, further decomposition occurs by platform and asset type (e.g., Find decomposes into SIGINT, IMINT, HUMINT, OSINT collection assets; Engage decomposes into air-delivered, surface-delivered, and cyber/electronic attack options). The decomposition is not recursive in the algorithmic sense — it is a fixed six-link chain, not a fractal structure.
granularity_guidance: The kill chain decomposes to the level of individual platforms, weapon systems, and intelligence disciplines. A 'leaf node' in F2T2EA terms is a specific sensor platform contributing to Find (e.g., an MQ-9 Reaper providing full-motion video), a specific weapon system contributing to Engage (e.g., a particular F-35 with a specific munition), or a specific BDA sensor (e.g., a national technical means satellite tasked for post-strike imagery). The stopping condition is pragmatic: decompose until every link in the chain has at least one assigned and available asset. If a link has no assigned asset, the chain is broken and the target cannot be engaged through this chain — either find an alternative chain or defer the target. The doctrine explicitly warns against over-decomposition: at some point, adding more granularity does not improve chain reliability and adds coordination overhead.
boundary_rules: The kill chain enforces clean boundaries through functional separation: each phase has clearly defined inputs, outputs, and responsible organizations. Find is owned by the ISR collection manager; Fix is owned by the intelligence analysis cell; Track is a shared responsibility between ISR and operations; Target is owned by the targeting cell under the JFC; Engage is owned by the executing unit; Assess is owned by the intelligence and operations staffs jointly. These functional boundaries are enforced by the Joint Operations Center battle rhythm: each phase has specific products that must be produced and handed off to the next phase, and handoff failures are the primary source of chain breakage. There is no MECE requirement — some activities (like sensor tasking) span multiple phases. The boundary between F2T2EA (dynamic targeting) and the Joint Targeting Cycle (deliberate targeting) is governed by time sensitivity: targets that can be planned in the 24-72 hour JTC cycle go through deliberate targeting; those requiring response within minutes to hours go through the dynamic F2T2EA kill chain.
risk_assessment_mechanism: Risk assessment in F2T2EA is multi-layered and integrated into each phase. (1) Collateral Damage Estimation (CDE): conducted in the Target phase using validated CDE methodology to estimate and mitigate civilian casualties and damage to protected sites. (2) Rules of Engagement compliance: every target is checked against current ROE, Law of War, and specific targeting directives. (3) Blue-on-Blue risk: tracking data is continuously assessed for friendly force proximity; engagement is blocked if friendlies are within the weapon's effects radius. (4) Chain reliability risk: assessors evaluate whether each link is likely to hold for the duration of the engagement window — if a link is degraded (e.g., sensor is task-saturated, communications are jammed), the risk of chain failure increases and the engagement may be deferred. (5) Weapon-target mismatch risk: weaponeering analysis assesses whether the selected weapon will achieve desired effects against the specific target type. (6) Escalation risk: diplomatic and political consequences of engagement are evaluated, particularly for targets in contested or politically sensitive areas. Risk classification uses a qualitative high/medium/low schema rather than a quantitative probabilistic model. Formal risk matrices are not standard doctrine but are used in practice by targeting cells.
unknown_handling: When the kill chain encounters unknowns, the response is phase-dependent and follows a graduated escalation model. (1) FIND unknown (unidentified contact): continue collecting to resolve ambiguity; do not proceed to Fix without validated detection. (2) FIX unknown (cannot classify target): request additional sensor coverage, cross-reference with intelligence databases; if still unknown, the target may be escalated to the J2 (intelligence) for deeper analysis or deferred until more data is available. (3) TRACK unknown (lost track): re-task sensors to re-acquire; if track cannot be re-established, the kill chain is broken and must restart at Find. (4) TARGET unknown (no viable engagement option): defer target, continue tracking, and escalate to higher headquarters for asset allocation or policy guidance. (5) ASSESS unknown (inconclusive BDA): authorize re-attack if target is high-priority and there is reason to believe it survived; otherwise, defer to the next JTC cycle for deliberate re-engagement. The default behavior is to HALT rather than proceed through unknowns — the kill chain's design philosophy is conservative, requiring positive confirmation before engaging. The commander can override this conservatism for high-priority TSTs.
default_stance_on_unknown: RESTRICTIVE (block until confirmed). The F2T2EA kill chain is designed to default-safe: you do not engage a target you cannot positively identify, track, and assess. This is driven by the catastrophic consequences of a wrong engagement (civilian casualties, fratricide, diplomatic incidents). The restrictive default is enforced through multiple layers: (1) Positive Identification (PID) requirements; (2) CDE methodology that gates engagement; (3) Commander's Decision authority that provides human override of the restrictive default. The restrictive stance can be deliberately relaxed for specific mission profiles (e.g., defensive counter-air engagements where any unidentified aircraft penetrating a defended zone may be engaged), but this is an explicit exception, not the default.
safety_guardrails: The F2T2EA kill chain has extensive safety guardrails, reflecting its life-and-death operational domain. (1) Positive Identification (PID): doctrine requires PID before engagement — the target must be visually or electronically identified as hostile. (2) Collateral Damage Estimation (CDE): standardized methodology for predicting and mitigating civilian harm; engagements with CDE above threshold require elevated approval. (3) No-Strike List (NSL) and Restricted Target List (RTL): legal and policy-based lists of protected entities that are automatically excluded from targeting. (4) Rules of Engagement (ROE): legal framework governing when and how force may be used; every engagement must comply. (5) Commander's Decision gate: human approval required before engagement; the human is a deliberate check in the chain. (6) Time-sensitive target (TST) procedures: while TSTs have compressed timelines, they do not bypass safety guardrails — the guardrails are accelerated, not eliminated. (7) Weapons safety: weapon-specific safety procedures (arm/safe, weapon release authority, abort procedures). (8) Combat Assessment verification: post-engagement BDA provides accountability. In an AI-agent context, equivalent guardrails would include: allowlists for authorized tool calls, output validation before execution, human-in-the-loop for destructive or irreversible actions, and kill switches that can halt agent execution.
human_decision_points: Humans are IN the loop at multiple critical decision points. (1) Target validation (Fix → Track transition): intelligence analysts validate that the target meets PID and ROE requirements — this is a human decision. (2) Engagement authorization (Target → Engage transition): the Commander's Decision gate requires a human commander or delegated targeting authority to approve the specific engagement. This is the most consequential human decision point. (3) Abort authority: the engagement can be aborted by the weapon system operator, the controlling agency, or the commander at any point up to weapon impact. (4) Re-attack authorization (Assess → Find loop): the decision to re-engage requires human approval. (5) TST designation: the commander designates which targets are Time-Sensitive Targets, triggering accelerated procedures. The human role is fundamentally IN the loop for engagement decisions — the system is designed to augment and accelerate human decision-making, not to replace it. The distinction between being 'in the loop' (directly making the decision) and 'on the loop' (overseeing AI-generated decisions) is the doctrinal tension that programs like Thunderforge are now navigating.
override_capability: Override capability exists at multiple levels. (1) The commander can override targeting recommendations, redirect the kill chain to a different target, or halt an engagement in progress. (2) The engagement authority can be delegated downward but remains revocable — a higher commander can rescind delegation. (3) The weapon system operator has abort authority independent of the command chain — if the operator observes a safety-of-flight issue or a last-second PID problem, they can abort regardless of prior authorization. (4) Override granularity ranges from full-halt (abort the entire operation) to per-target (reassign this specific target) to per-action (abort this specific engagement while leaving other engagements active). In practice, the 'per-engagement abort' is the most important override mechanism, exercised by the operator in the final seconds before weapon impact. The system is designed with multiple independent abort paths (belt-and-suspenders) reflecting the catastrophic consequences of a failure to abort.
fallback_when_no_human: F2T2EA doctrine assumes continuous human availability; there is no provision for autonomous engagement without human authorization. If a human is unavailable (e.g., communications are lost between the targeting cell and the weapon system), the default is to HALT and not engage — the kill chain breaks. For certain pre-planned missions, weapons release authority may be pre-delegated to the executing unit commander, but this is still human decision-making, just at a different echelon. In practice, communications loss during the Target phase means the engagement is scrubbed; communications loss during Engage may mean the weapon continues on its last valid guidance (for fire-and-forget munitions) or self-destructs (for command-guided munitions). The timeout-and-default for human unavailability is always DEFAULT-SAFE: do not engage without positive human authorization. This doctrinal fact is precisely the tension that Thunderforge is designed to navigate — maintaining safety while compressing the human decision timeline.
max_problem_size: F2T2EA has been demonstrated on operations involving hundreds of simultaneous targets across multiple combatant commands, with the 2003 Iraq War 'Shock and Awe' campaign representing the largest historical test (thousands of aimpoints prosecuted over 48 hours). The theoretical limit is constrained by three factors: (1) sensor availability — you can only Fix and Track as many targets as you have sensors to cover; (2) command bandwidth — the Commander's Decision gate becomes a bottleneck when hundreds of targets require human approval; (3) deconfliction complexity — as the number of simultaneous engagements increases, the risk of unintended interactions (mid-air collisions, frequency interference, fratricide) grows super-linearly. What breaks at scale: (a) the human commander becomes a serial bottleneck, leading to delegated engagement authority (which trades safety for speed); (b) the common operational picture becomes congested, degrading situational awareness; (c) BDA assignment becomes impossible when too many engagements are assessed simultaneously. NATO and U.S. doctrine are actively working on these scaling limits through automated target recognition, AI-assisted BDA, and dynamic deconfliction algorithms.
parallel_execution: Parallelism is first-class and essential to the kill chain's operational value. Multiple kill chains can execute concurrently against different targets, with each chain operating independently. Dependencies are managed through: (1) the Air Tasking Order (ATO), which deconflicts airspace, frequencies, and asset assignments across parallel chains; (2) the common operational picture, which ensures parallel chains don't interfere (e.g., two chains targeting the same entity); (3) asset scheduling, which prevents two chains from simultaneously tasking the same sensor or weapon platform. The parallelism is not bolted-on — it is inherent in the concept: the kill chain is designed so that a single command-and-control infrastructure can manage many concurrent chains. The limiting factor on parallelism is not the chain structure but asset availability: you can only run as many concurrent chains as you have sensors, shooters, and command bandwidth to support. Modern doctrine (JADC2) is pushing toward 'kill webs' rather than 'kill chains' — dynamically reconfigurable networks where any sensor can feed any shooter, increasing parallelism by eliminating fixed chain-to-asset binding.
team_scaling_model: Sub-linear to negative scaling beyond a force-size threshold. Adding more personnel to the kill chain improves performance up to a point by enabling specialization (dedicated Find analysts, dedicated BDA analysts). Beyond that point, coordination overhead and handoff latency degrade performance. The Joint Targeting School teaches that the optimal targeting cell size is approximately 15-25 personnel covering all six phases, with additional personnel providing depth (shift rotations) rather than breadth (more parallel chains). The negative scaling after the threshold is driven by: (1) increased handoff errors as more people touch the targeting product; (2) information siloing where the left hand doesn't know what the right hand is tracking; (3) decision paralysis as more stakeholders demand consultation before engagement. The doctrinal solution is to scale by replication (multiple independent targeting cells) rather than by expanding individual cells.
cost_scaling_model: Operational cost scales roughly linearly with the number of targets engaged, but with a significant fixed infrastructure cost (the ISR, C2, and BDA architecture must exist regardless of how many targets are engaged). Per-target marginal cost is driven by: (1) sensor time (the most constrained and expensive resource); (2) weapon expenditure (precision munitions are expensive); (3) BDA collection (post-strike sensor tasking). The branching factor is low — each target typically generates one engagement, with re-attack occurring for approximately 15-30% of targets (based on historical combat data). Cost is dominated by the high fixed cost of the ISR and C2 infrastructure, making the kill chain economically viable only when engaging a sufficient volume of targets to amortize the fixed cost. In an AI-agent context, the computational cost would scale roughly linearly with the number of targets, as each target requires its own set of LLM inference calls for analysis, targeting, and assessment.
replanning_built_in: The kill chain has replanning built in as a first-class concern through the Assess phase and the re-attack loop. If an engagement fails (target not defeated), the chain re-enters at Find to re-acquire the target and re-engage. This is not a global replan — it is a local compensation: only the specific link that failed is re-executed, not the entire operation. If the failure is systemic (e.g., the weapon type is ineffective against this target class), the Target phase must select a different engagement option in the next iteration. The re-attack loop is explicitly designed and time-bounded: after a certain number of unsuccessful re-attacks or after the target window closes, the target is escalated to deliberate targeting (the Joint Targeting Cycle) rather than continuing dynamic re-engagement. This prevents infinite re-attack loops that consume assets without effect.
trigger_mechanism: Adaptation within the kill chain is triggered by: (1) Engagement failure — BDA shows the target survived or was insufficiently degraded, triggering re-attack; (2) Target window change — the target maneuvers, enters protected airspace, or reaches sanctuary, requiring a revised engagement timeline; (3) Asset loss — the assigned weapon system or sensor becomes unavailable (mechanical failure, tasking conflict, enemy action), triggering a search for an alternative chain; (4) Commander re-prioritization — a higher-priority target emerges, causing reallocation of assets; (5) Environmental change — weather, communications degradation, or electromagnetic interference degrades a link. The trigger is always event-based, not time-based — the kill chain does not periodically replan; it replans only when a link breaks or a higher-priority demand emerges.
adaptation_cost: Adaptation within F2T2EA is relatively cheap for local adjustments and expensive for global changes. Local compensation (re-task a sensor, switch to a backup weapon) costs seconds to minutes and preserves the rest of the chain's state. Global replan (target completely re-enters the kill chain from Find) costs minutes to hours and loses all prior state — the target must be re-found, re-fixed, and re-tracked from scratch. The most expensive adaptation is the loss of a target window due to slow replanning — a time-sensitive target that escapes because the kill chain couldn't reconfigure fast enough is a permanent, irrecoverable loss. This creates a tension: replanning too quickly risks a bad engagement decision; replanning too slowly loses the target. Doctrine addresses this through pre-planned alternative engagement options (branches and sequels) that reduce adaptation cost by having contingencies ready.
failure_recovery_mechanism: The kill chain has layered failure recovery. (1) Link-level recovery: if a single link degrades, the system compensates locally. Lost sensor → re-task a different sensor. Lost track → re-acquire. Weapon malfunction → switch to backup weapon or abort. BDA inconclusive → supplement with alternative intelligence sources. (2) Chain-level recovery: if the chain breaks entirely (e.g., communications blackout), the target is deferred and the chain restarts from Find when conditions allow. (3) Blast radius containment: the kill chain's sequential design naturally contains failures — a failure in Fix does not affect parallel chains targeting different entities. (4) Graceful degradation: if the full six-link chain cannot be executed (e.g., no BDA assets available), the commander may accept a truncated chain (engage without full BDA) for high-priority targets, explicitly accepting the risk of unknown combat assessment. (5) Human escalation: any link failure that cannot be resolved at the operator level is escalated to the targeting cell chief, then the J3 (Operations), then the JFC. (6) Doctrine mandates no single point of failure — each link should have redundant assets available, though in practice sensor and shooter shortages create de facto single points of failure.
output_artifacts: The F2T2EA process produces: (1) Target Nomination List (TNL) or dynamic target nomination — the formal nomination of a time-sensitive target for engagement; (2) Target Intelligence Package — the compiled intelligence on the target including imagery, signals data, and analytical assessments; (3) Collateral Damage Estimate (CDE) — the formal CDE product with methodology and mitigation recommendations; (4) Air Tasking Order (ATO) / Fragmentary Order (FRAGO) — the tasking document that assigns the engagement to a specific unit; (5) Mission Report (MISREP) — the post-engagement report from the executing unit; (6) Battle Damage Assessment (BDA) product — imagery and analysis of post-strike target condition; (7) Munitions Effectiveness Assessment (MEA) — weapon system performance evaluation; (8) Re-attack Recommendation (RR) — formal recommendation to re-engage or close the target.
briefing_format: The kill chain status is communicated through: (1) the Common Operational Picture (COP) — a real-time visual display showing all active kill chains, target locations, asset status, and engagement timelines; (2) the Joint Targeting Coordination Board (JTCB) brief — a standardized briefing format covering priority targets, chain status, resource conflicts, and commander's decisions required; (3) Fragmentary Orders (FRAGO) for time-sensitive retasking; (4) daily Air Tasking Order (ATO) for planned engagements. The compression format is highly visual and status-driven: green/amber/red status indicators for each link in each active chain, with drill-down into detail for amber/red status items. This is optimized for commander decision-making under time pressure — the commander needs to know 'which chains are red and what do you need me to do about it?' not a detailed narrative.
traceability: The kill chain has strong traceability by design, because engagement legality and accountability demand it. (1) Every engagement is documented with the target nomination, the intelligence basis for the targeting decision, the CDE, the commander's approval, the execution record, and the BDA. (2) This creates a complete audit trail from decision to outcome. (3) The Modernized Integrated Database (MIDB) maintains the authoritative record of target intelligence and engagement history. (4) Combat Assessment provides the feedback loop that validates or questions the initial targeting assumptions. (5) In the event of a civilian casualty incident or ROE violation, this traceability is essential for the legal and investigative process. In an AI-agent context, this level of traceability (every decision linked to its evidence basis and approval chain) is the gold standard for agentic systems — the kill chain shows that comprehensive traceability is achievable when the stakes demand it.
agent_communication_protocol: In the military context, kill chain communication uses: (1) Link 16 — a tactical data link for real-time sensor and track data sharing between platforms; (2) Joint Range Extension (JRE) — extending tactical data links beyond line-of-sight; (3) voice communications over secure nets for command and coordination; (4) chat and messaging systems (mIRC, JChat) for non-real-time coordination; (5) the Joint Automated Deep Operations Coordination System (JADOCS) for fires coordination. These are military-specific protocols, not general-purpose agent communication protocols. In an AI-agent context, the kill chain pattern maps to: (a) a message bus for inter-agent communication (analogous to Link 16), (b) structured message formats for target nominations, tasking orders, and assessments (analogous to J-series messages), (c) synchronous real-time communication for time-sensitive handoffs, and (d) asynchronous communication for non-time-sensitive coordination. MCP or A2A would be the natural protocol choices for AI-agent kill chain implementations, with the key requirement being low-latency, reliable message delivery with positive acknowledgment.
learning_memory_architecture: The kill chain itself has limited built-in learning — it is fundamentally an execution framework, not a learning framework. Learning happens through separate but coupled processes: (1) the Joint Lessons Learned Program captures engagement outcomes and feeds them into doctrine updates; (2) the Weapons System Effectiveness Program evaluates weapon performance and updates weaponeering databases; (3) the AAR process (separately documented) captures tactical lessons from specific engagements; (4) the Joint Targeting School updates training based on operational experience. The kill chain doctrine itself is updated through the JP 3-60 revision cycle. There is no real-time, per-engagement learning mechanism — each kill chain execution is treated as largely independent of previous executions. This is a recognized gap: the concept of a 'learning kill chain' where assessment data from one engagement automatically updates targeting models for subsequent engagements is an active area of research and development, and is central to the Thunderforge program's vision.
latency_bounds: F2T2EA operates under hard real-time or near-real-time constraints depending on the target window. For air-to-air engagements, the entire kill chain must execute in seconds to minutes. For time-sensitive ground targets, the window may be minutes to hours. The doctrine does not specify hard latency targets — it specifies that the chain must execute within the target window, which is target-specific. Degradation under time pressure: as time compresses, the chain short-circuits. The PID standard may be relaxed (from visual ID to electronic ID), CDE methodology is accelerated, and engagement authority is pushed to lower echelons. This is not 'graceful degradation' in the engineering sense — it is deliberate risk acceptance. The acknowledged trade-off is that compressed kill chains have higher rates of misidentification and collateral damage. Thunderforge's goal is to compress the chain without degrading decision quality by automating the time-intensive analysis steps (PID, CDE, weaponeering) while preserving human judgment on the engagement decision.
evaluation_benchmarking: No standardized public benchmarks exist for kill chain performance — the domain is classified and performance evaluation is conducted through: (1) operational mission reports (MISREPs) documenting engagement outcomes; (2) Combat Assessment products comparing desired effects to achieved effects; (3) weapons system effectiveness reports (WSERs); (4) training exercises (Red Flag, Weapons School, Fleet Synthetic Training) that evaluate kill chain performance in simulated environments; (5) wargaming simulations (JSAF, JTLS) that model kill chain dynamics. Key classified metrics include: chain completion rate (what percentage of initiated kill chains result in engagement), time from Find to Engage, re-attack rate, collateral damage rate, and BDA accuracy. In an unclassified LLM-agent context, evaluation would need to be constructed from first principles — there is no F2T2EA-specific agent benchmark.
mapping_strength: F2T2EA maps strongly to multi-agent software workflows in surprising ways. (1) The six-phase structure directly parallels a CI/CD pipeline with monitoring: Find = error detection / alerting, Fix = triage and classification, Track = monitoring and observability, Target = remediation planning, Engage = deployment/fix, Assess = post-deployment validation. (2) In an LLM-agent context: Find = the orchestrator detects a task needing execution or a failure condition; Fix = a research agent classifies the problem; Track = the orchestrator monitors task progress in real-time; Target = the planning agent selects the best execution approach; Engage = the execution agent performs the action; Assess = a verification agent validates the result and recommends rework if needed. (3) The handoff gates map naturally to structured validation between agent phases. (4) The distributed decision architecture (different agents owning different phases) maps well to a multi-agent system with specialized agents. (5) The re-attack loop maps to retry logic with escalation. The primary translation needed is from physical/kinetic targeting to digital/informational targeting — 'target' becomes 'task', 'weapon' becomes 'tool' or 'action', 'BDA' becomes 'output validation'.
gaps: Several aspects of F2T2EA do not transfer cleanly to software/agent contexts. (1) Physical constraint dependence: the kill chain's logic is deeply tied to physical constraints (sensor range, weapon flight time, geography) that have no analog in software. (2) Adversarial assumption: F2T2EA assumes an intelligent, actively resisting adversary; most software/agent workflows are cooperative or indifferent, not adversarial. (3) Irreversibility: engagement decisions in F2T2EA are often irreversible (a weapon launched cannot be un-launched); software actions are generally reversible or have rollback mechanisms. (4) Legal/ROE overlay: the kill chain is governed by a dense legal and policy framework with no equivalent in civilian software. (5) Human-on-the-loop model: F2T2EA has humans deeply in the loop for engagement decisions, which may not be practical or necessary for many software tasks. (6) The 'kill' metaphor: the framework is optimized for destructive engagement against discrete targets, which constrains its applicability to constructive or creative tasks. (7) Fixed six-link structure: not all problems fit a six-phase pipeline; the structure is doctrinally fixed rather than dynamically determined by the problem.
adaptation_required: To apply F2T2EA to the Jordan pipeline: (1) Rename phases to better fit the domain: Find = detect task, Fix = analyze requirements, Track = monitor dependencies, Target = plan approach, Engage = execute, Assess = validate output. (2) Replace the 'commander's decision' gate with Jordan's existing approval gate mechanism — the human approval point in F2T2EA maps directly to Jordan's HITL checkpoints. (3) Implement the re-attack/retry loop as a first-class feature: when validation fails, the system should loop back through a lightweight version of the chain (re-analyze, re-plan, re-execute) rather than aborting or requiring full re-planning. (4) Use the chain's link status model (green/amber/red per phase) for pipeline observability — a dashboard showing the health of each phase for each active task. (5) The 'target window' concept maps to task deadlines: some tasks have hard deadlines (analogous to target windows), and the pipeline should prioritize based on window proximity. (6) Adapt the collateral damage estimation concept to 'side effect estimation' — before executing a task, estimate what else might be affected and whether those effects are acceptable. (7) The kill chain's serial design should be adapted to support branching (parallel execution paths for independent subtasks) while maintaining the sequential discipline for dependent phases.
analogous_jordan_node: plan (primary), execute (primary), approval_gate (secondary). The Target phase maps to plan (selecting the approach), the Engage phase maps to execute (performing the action), and the Commander's Decision gate maps to approval_gate.
validation_context: F2T2EA has been validated through: (1) over 30 years of combat operations from Desert Storm (1991) through current operations; (2) extensive testing at Air Force Weapons School, Navy Strike Fighter Weapons School (TOPGUN), and Joint Targeting School; (3) major exercises including Red Flag, Bold Quest, and Joint Expeditionary Force Experiment (JEFX); (4) real-world time-sensitive targeting operations in Iraq and Afghanistan (2001-2021), where the F2T2EA kill chain was used to prosecute thousands of dynamic targets; (5) NATO interoperability exercises validating the kill chain across allied forces; (6) continuous refinement through the Joint Lessons Learned Program. The kill chain is one of the most operationally validated frameworks in military doctrine, though its performance metrics are largely classified. F2T2EA has also seen limited adoption in cybersecurity (the 'cyber kill chain' concept developed by Lockheed Martin adapts F2T2EA to intrusion detection and response).
known_failures: Documented failure modes: (1) Chain breakage at handoffs — the most common failure; intelligence fails to pass target data to operations in time, or the ATO cycle is too slow for the target window. (2) Misidentification — the target is fixed as hostile when it is actually friendly, neutral, or civilian; the 2003 Patriot fratricide incidents are canonical examples. (3) Target window miscalculation — the engagement is planned for a window that closes before execution, wasting assets and exposing the operation. (4) BDA overconfidence — BDA declares the target destroyed when it was not (or vice versa), leading to either unnecessary re-attacks or failure to re-engage a surviving target. (5) Kill chain fixation — the organization becomes so focused on executing established kill chains that it fails to detect when the enemy has shifted to a new operating pattern that the chain cannot address. (6) Sensor saturation — too many targets in the Find/Fix pipeline overwhelm sensor and analyst capacity, causing critical targets to be missed. (7) Communications degradation — jamming or terrain masking breaks the data links between chain nodes, collapsing the distributed kill chain into isolated nodes that cannot coordinate. (8) Escalation spiral — a kill chain executed against a target in contested space triggers an unintended escalation, a risk amplified when AI-assisted kill chains misjudge the political context.
where_still_used: Active use: (1) All U.S. Joint Force operations — F2T2EA is the doctrinal standard for dynamic targeting across all services. (2) U.S. Air Force Air Operations Centers (AOCs) worldwide. (3) U.S. Navy Carrier Strike Groups and amphibious ready groups. (4) U.S. Marine Corps fires coordination centers. (5) U.S. Army fires brigades and division artillery headquarters. (6) NATO Allied Joint Force Commands — interoperable targeting doctrine. (7) Five Eyes intelligence and targeting integration. (8) Joint Targeting School (Dam Neck, Virginia) — continuing to train targeting personnel. (9) Cybersecurity adaptation — the Lockheed Martin Cyber Kill Chain framework (reconnaissance, weaponization, delivery, exploitation, installation, C2, actions on objectives) is directly inspired by F2T2EA and widely used in cybersecurity operations centers. (10) Active doctrinal evolution — JADC2 and CJADC2 are evolving the 'kill chain' into the 'kill web' (networked, resilient, dynamically reconfigurable), reflecting lessons from F2T2EA's known limitations.
